summary:
One of the most striking aspects of [[event:4|Natufian culture]] is the evidence for long-distance exchange of prestige and ornamental goods, particularly marine shells used as personal ornaments. Dentalium (tusk shell), Spondylus (thorny oyster), and Nassarius gibbosulus shells — sourced from the Mediterranean coast and sometimes the Red Sea — are found at Natufian sites well into the interior of the Levant, sometimes hundreds of kilometers from their origin.

These shell ornaments appear in Natufian burials and as body decoration, demonstrating that exchange networks existed connecting coastal and inland communities before the adoption of agriculture. The long-distance shell trade of the Natufian culture is part of a broader pattern of prehistoric shell exchange documented across the Mediterranean and Near East from at least 130,000 BCE onward, but the Natufian examples are particularly significant as evidence for the elaboration of exchange networks accompanying the transition to sedentism — a transition in which access to prestige goods and the social relationships underlying their exchange played important roles. The trade networks demonstrated by Natufian shell ornaments became the foundation for the more elaborate exchange systems of the subsequent Neolithic period.
